The 'destroyer' was actually the submarine Fratelli Bandiera (T.V. (Lt.) C. Forni). She tried to attack with torpedoes and gun but the enemy submarine dived too quickly (40 seconds) and contact was lost. (21)
5 Oct 1941 Around 1630 B/5, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

14 Oct 1941 Around 1810 A/14, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south-east coast of Sicily, in position 128° - Cape Murro di Porco - 9.5 nautical miles, to intercept three Italian destroyers of the Navigatori-class there are thought to make a high speed run to North Africa with supplies.

This referred to the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno (310 troops), Antonio Da Noli (295 troops) and Nicolò Zeno (268 troops) which sailed from Augusta at 1830 B/15 - 1850 B/15. They were transporting German troops to Benghazi where they arrived at 1630 B/16 - 2030 B/16.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina and like HMS Urge, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) was also ordered to sea for a brief patrol to intercept the enemy.

15 Oct 1941 At 0850 A/15, well to the south-east of Cape Murro di Porco in approximate position 36°45'N 15°34'E, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) sighted a southbound hospital ship proceeding on course 190°. It was thought to be the Virgilio (Italian, 11718 GRT, built 1928).
This was indeed the Virgilio which had sailed at 0730 B/15 from Augusta for Tripoli.
At 2200 A/15, nothing had been sighted, HMS Urge left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with her patrol orders. (21)
16 Oct 1941 Around 1130 A/16, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

18 Oct 1941 Around 1530 A/18, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the East coast of Tunisia between Kuriat and Lampion Island in Area 'K'. (35°18'N, 11°11'E - 345° - 30 - 60)
19 Oct 1941 At 1105 A/19, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) sighted a schooner entering Lampedusa harbour. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1900 A/19, Urge entered her patrol area and continued on to the westward. (21)

20 Oct 1941 At 2220 A/20, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) received Capt. S 10's signal timed 1645 A/20 which reported a northbound enemy convoy of a transport, a tanker and three destroyers as escort. HMS Urge was to patrol in position 35°40'N, 12°07'E. It was expected the convoy would pass through this position around 0800 A/21.
In a signal timed 2105 A/20, Capt. S 10's ordered HMS Urge to patrol in position 35°40'N, 11°54'E, where it was now expected the convoy would pass through around 0900 A/21.

These signals referred to the convoy made up of the tanker Proserpina (Italian, former French Beauce, 4869 GRT, built 1926) and the transport Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1925) escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco, Folgore and Antoniotto Usodimare. They had departed from Tripoli at 1555 B/20-1730 B/20 for Naples but were ordered back when it was learned that an enemy force of two cruisers and two destroyers was at sea. They returned to Tripoli at 1320 B/21-1325 B/21.

The enemy force referred to by the Italians was ' Force K ', made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyers HMS Lance (Lt.Cdr. R.W.F. Northcott, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) which were on passage from Gibraltar to Malta. (25)

22 Oct 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) attacked the small Italian merchant vessel Falco (343 GRT, built 1919) near Kuriat in position 35°45'N, 11°06'E. According to Italian sources she was unharmed, though one torpedo apparently hit the ship but was deflected without exploding and another torpedo passed underneath. This caused the crew to abandon ship for some time.

The other vessels in the convoy were the motor auxiliary sailing vessel Illiria escorted by the minesweepers Angelo Musco (R.26), Italia (G.90) and Maria di Piedigrotta (R.122). They had sailed from Pantelleria for Tripoli. Illaria and/or Italia dropped a few depth charges.

Later on this day HMS Urge also torpedoed and further damaged the Italian merchant bessel Marigola (5996 GRT, built 1906) off Kuriat, Tunisia in position 35°50'N, 11°06'E. The Marigola was already grounded after being torpedoed by aircraft on 24 September 1941. Five of her crew were slightly wounded, she was later raised by the French.

0630 B/22 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 1500 tons with the escort of one large 3-masted schooner and four small auxiliary schooners. All these schooners were armed. Started attack on the merchant vessel.

0700 B/22 - Fired three torpedoes. No hits were obtained but the ship turned towards the shore. The crew was then seen to abandon her taking to the schooners. The schooners then started to patrol to seaward.
0756 B/22 - Urge meanwhile had been brought round. A single torpedo was then fired from 3000 yards. This torpedo was heard to hit after just over 2 minutes.

1042 B/22 - Sighted a merchant ship of about 4000 tons of Kuriat. Proceeded to the north to investigate but it was impossible to get close enough while dived. Decided to wait until dusk and then to proceed close in on the surface.
1815 B/22 - Surfaced and closed.
1834 B/22 - Fired one torpedo set to 8 feet from 3700 yards.
1837 B/22 - The torpedo hit amidships and a black column of smoke rose 100 feet into the air. (21)

26 Oct 1941 At 1630 A/26, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) received Capt. S 10's signal timed 1135 A/26 recalling her from patrol. She was ordered to proceed through position 35°45'N, 12°40'E. Course was immediately set to return to Malta. (25)

28 Oct 1941 Around 0630 B/28, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. On the return passage to Malta a fuel leak had been discovered. About 10 tons of fuel had been lost due to this leak. A docking was required to repair the leak. (21)
1 Nov 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) is docked in No.1 dock at Malta. (26)
6 Nov 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) is undocked. (26)

8 Nov 1941 Around 1730 B/8, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea in position 37°50'N, 19°27'E.
En-route she was to proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E at 2200 B/9.

9 Nov 1941 At 2030 B/9, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1813 B/9, which had information on two damaged enemy merchant vessels either of which might be attacked.
At 1341 B/9, a damaged merchant ship had been seen in position 37°16'N, 17°56'E. She was laying stopped.
At 1456 B/9, another damaged merchant ship had been seen in position 37°26'N, 18°06'E. This ship was proceeding on course 095° at 3 knots.
On receiving the signal a searchlight was seen bearing 050°. Urge altered course to 040° to intercept and get down moon from it.

At 2310 B/9, Capt. S.10's signal timed 2103 B/9 was received which ordered HMS Urge to patrol in position 'M' (37°41'5"N, 19°37'5"E). Course was set according and Urge proceeded on course 069° to proceed to the position ordered.

The ships sighted were ships from the Beta convoy / Duisburg convoy which was decimated by the cruisers and destroyers of Force K operating from Malta shortly of midnight. The convoy had been made up of the merchant vessels Duisburg (German, 7389 GRT, built 1928) Maria (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1926), Minatitlan (Italian (tanker), 7599 GRT, built 1941), Sagitta (Italian, 5153 GRT, built 1919) and San Marco (German (former French), 3113 GRT, built 1931). Off Messina these ships had been joined by two more merchant vessels the Conte di Misurata (Italian (tanker), 5014 GRT, built 1908) and Rina Corrado (Italian, 5180 GRT, built 1918).
The convoy had been escorted by a close escort made up of the Italian destroyers Maestrale (SO), Euro, Fulmine, Grecale, Libeccio and Oriani. (21)

10 Nov 1941 At 0230 B/10, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) saw that the searchlight previously sighted was from a hospital ship obviously searching for survivors of the convoy which had been decimated by ' Force K ' the previous night. Course was altered to pass south of this hospital ship and keep clear of the searchlight.

This was most probably the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926) which was searching for survivors until 1630/11. Another possibility is the Italian Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) but she seems to have already left the scene by this time.
Between 0330 and 0530 B/10 a lot of fuel and wreckage were seen floating on the surface.

At 0530 B/10, a burning ship was sighted about 15 nautical miles away to the south-east. She continued to burn all through the day and was still burning the next night. This may have been the wreck of the Italian tanker Minatitlán (7651 GRT, built 1941). Aircraft which were searching for survivors were sighted throughout the day. (21)
11 Nov 1941 At 0230 B/11, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol position (37°41'5"N, 19°37'5"E) (21)

14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1006 B/14, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) to shift the patrol line 053 - 27 miles to be in position to intercept two fast southbound supply ships.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the troop transports (naval auxiliaries) Città di Genova (Italian, 5413 GRT, built 1930) and Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1929) which were to proceed from Taranto to Benghasi escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Giovanni da Verrazzano.
The position of HMS Urge to intercept this convoy was the result of an ULTRA intercept. [Code breaking of the enemy's naval cypher, source: file DEFE3/690/462, (National Archives, London)]. (27)
14 Nov 1941 At 1600 B/14, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) arrived in the ordered position (37°59'N, 20°06'E). (21)

15 Nov 1941 At 1200 B/15, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) altered course to 233° to regain her original patrol position as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1055 B/14. Nothing had been seen of the enemy convoy though patrolling enemy aircraft had been sighted.
At 2100 B/15, HMS Urge was back in her original patrol position (37°41'5"N, 19°37'5"E). (21)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1635 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) proceed as ordered in the signal timed 1221 B/16. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) proceed towards Cephalonia. HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) are to remain on patrol. Further instructions will follow. (27)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1731 B/16 the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Important southbound convoy sighted off Brindisi at 1600 B/16 probably passing westward of Cephalonia and Zante (Zakinthos) daylight hours 17th. Establish a patrol line forthwith. 4 - 38°01'N, 20°13'E - 280° - 12 - 190° - 30. Positions to be taken up as follows: ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) in position 'A', 38°01'N, 20°13'E, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) in position 'B', HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) in position 'D'. Until further orders submarines are not to be attacked. Submarines in conjunction endeavour to establish SS/T communication when safe to do so. (27)

17 Nov 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) made a torpedo attack on a convoy about 40 nautical miles west of Cephalonia Island, Greece. No hits were obtained, most likely due to an error in firing intervals.

This was most probably the convoy made up of the transport vessels Tinos (German, 2826 GRT, built 1914) and the Bolsena (Italian, 2384 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the torpedo boat Orione on their way to Benghazi via Navarino.
This attack was the result of an ULTRA signal referring tot the Tinos/Bolsena convoy. [See files DEFE3/690/485 & 486 and DEFE3/834/1028 from the National Archives at London].
0130 B/17 - On receipt of Capt. S.10's signal timed 1731 B/16, HMS Urge set course to proceed to position 38°03'N, 19°58'E. A new patrol was set up to intercept a southbound enemy convoy.

The attack by HMS Urge was apparently unobserved. The convoy was attacked by six Blenheim bombers at 1450 B/17 in position 37°49'N, 19°45'E from very low altitude (50 metres). The aircraft dropped bombs but these missed their targets.
1225 B/17 - Sighted two southbound merchant ships of about 3500 tons escorted by one destroyer bearing 300°, distance approximately 8 nautical miles. Started attack.
1302 B/17 - In position 37°57'N, 19°47'E fired three torpedoes at the rear ship from about 5000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1310 B/17 - Four depth charges were dropped.
1325 B/17 - The convoy appeared to proceed undamaged.
1644 B/17 - Surfaced to make an enemy report.
1651 B/17 - Dived and returned to the ordered patrol position. (21)

18 Nov 1941 At 2340 B/18, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) received Capt. S.1's signal timed 1641 A/18, ordering her to return to Malta [See a second event for this day for the text of this signal]. (21)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1641 A/18, the Capt. S.1 cancelled the orders as had been given in his signal timed 1731 B/16. New orders were as follows; ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) proceeding to Navarino as had already been ordered in a signal timed 1113 B/18. HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) returning Malta forthwith through positions 38°13'N, 20°00'E, 38°18'N, 19°21'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E. HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) was to proceed forthwith to position 37°37'N, 19°38'E to a new patrol position that will be signalled in a later signal. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) was to remain north of 37°50'N until 0200 B/19 and then proceed through positions 37°29'N, 20°00'E and 36°27'N, 22°00'E to establish patrol in the southern Aegean. Submarines are not to be attacked during the night of the 18th/l9th. (27)
21 Nov 1941 Around 0800 B/21, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)
6 Dec 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) is docked in No.1 dock at Malta. (28)
9 Dec 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) is undocked. (28)

10 Dec 1941 Around 1400 B/10, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Strait of Messina in area 'F' (37°30'N, 15°09°E - 000° - 40 - 30).
11 Dec 1941 Around 1045 B/11, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) arrived in position 37°55'N, 15°30'E and commenced patrolling in the vicinty. (21)

12 Dec 1941 At 1055 B/12, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) went deep for an aircraft which dropped a bomb fairly close at 1108 B/12 and another one at 1111 B/12. It was considered possible HMS Urge had been detected at periscope depth.

At 1123 B/12, when HMS Urge returned to periscope depth two large transports were sighted escorted by two destroyers. They were proceeding southwards at about 16 knots keeping close to the Calabrian Coast and thus out of attacking range. During the day several patrolling aircraft as well as two A/S schooners were sighted. An enemy report on the two transports was passed at 2230 B/12.

The transports were the Fabio Filzi (6836 GRT, built 1940) and Carlo del Greco (6837 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare. They were on passage from Messina to Taranto. This convoy was attacked by HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) the following day which sank both transports near Taranto. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza was also present but was apparently not seen by HMS Urge. Dezza had sailed from Messina at 1000 B/12 and returned to harbour at 0130 B/13. She apparently, escorted the convoy to roughly south of Crotone and was not present when HMS Upright attacked it at 0212 B/13.
At 1925 B/12, HMS Urge surfaced near a motor torpedo boat which was laying stopped. Urge dived again and withdrew to the southeast surfacing again at 2030 B/12. (21)

14 Dec 1941 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian battleship Vittorio Veneto about 10 nautical miles west-south-west of Capo dell'Armi in position 37°52'N, 15°30'E.

The force attacked by HMS Urge was made up of the Italian battleships Vittorio Veneto and Littorio. They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino as well as the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Clio. Vittorio Veneto was hit on the port side creating a hole 9 m x 14 m under the rear 15” turret, 40 men were killed and 16 were slightly wounded. She still managed to make 21 knots and reach Taranto.

The Vittorio Veneto and Littorio and their escort, Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino sailed from Naples at 1620 B/13-1750 B/13. They were to provide distant cover to important convoys (see below). At 2300 B/13, Supermarina informed the battleship squadron that the departures of the convoys was delayed (they sailed on the 16th) and ordered them to proceed to Taranto. At 0730 B/14-0734 B/14, the torpedo boats Centauro and Clio sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort (and returned to Messina at 0249 B/15-0255 B/15). After the torpedoing, Vittorio Veneto and Littorio and the four destroyers arrived at Taranto at 2314 B/14-0054 B/15.

At 1530 B/13-2042 B/13, the battleship Caio Duilio, heavy cruiser Gorizia, the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Maestrale, Alfredo Oriani, Vincenzo Gioberti, Carabiniere, Corazziere and Nicolò Zeno sailed from Taranto. They were to provide close cover to the convoy made up of the transports Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1939) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the italian destroyers Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno and the italian torpedo boat Pegaso. The convoy sailed from Taranto at 1848 B/13 for Tripoli. At midnight, Caio Duilio, returning to base, ordered the destroyers Maestrale, Corazziere Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani to reinforce the Vittorio Veneto squadron. At 0407 B/14, Maestrale sighted a submarine 7 miles south of Punta Alice. The Caio Duilio squadron screened only by Carabiniere, arrived at Taranto at 0250 B/14-0400 B/14 (Zeno arrived only at 0900 B/14, perhaps carrying an A/S search off Taranto). The Monginevro convoy returned to Taranto at 0625 B/14-0642 B/14.

At 1700 B/13-2042 B/13, the battleship Andrea Doria, light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Muzio Attendolo escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio Da Noli and Nicoloso Da Recco. They were to provide close cover to the convoy made up of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta. This convoy sailed from Taranto at 1255 B/13 from Taranto for Benghazi. Following the order to postpone the operation, the convoy returned to Taranto at 1715 B/14. The Andrea Doria squadron returned to Taranto at 0157 B/14-0308 B/14.

A third convoy sailed from Argostoli (originally from Brindisi) at 1610 B/13 for Benghazi. It was made up of the Italian transports Iseo (2366 GRT, built 1918) and Capo Orso (3149 GRT, built 1916) escorted by the destroyers Strale and Turbine. Following the order to postpone the operation, the convoy turned back, but both merchant ships collided, causing serious damage, Capo Orso had to be towed, they arrived at Argostoli at 0845 B/14.

0840 B/14 - Came to periscope depth after hearing HE. Sighted two battleships, thought to be of the Cavour-class, screened by four destroyers proceeding southwards through the Strait of Messina at 17 knots. Started attack.
0858 B/14 - Fired four torpedoes at the rear battleship from 3000 yards.
0901 B/14 - Heard two (possibly three) explosions at the correct running range. A counter attack in which 40 depth charges were dropped followed during the next half hour.
1000 B/14- Commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.
1240 B/14 - Made an enemy report twice on the mast aerial.
2255 B/14 - Encountered two motor torpedo boats. Dived to evade detection. (21)

15 Dec 1941 At 0145 B/15, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) received Capt. S 10's signal timed 2153 B/14 which ordered her to patrol in 'area F', 37°30'N, 15°09°E, 000° - 40 - 30. She was to concentrate on position 37°51'N, 15°37'E. Course was set to comply with these orders.
During the day Urge patrolled near position 37°51'N, 15°37'E. (29)

17 Dec 1941 At 0045 B/17, south of Cape dell Armi, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) heard two motor torpedo boats transmitting on Asdics and hunting. This went on until 0150 B/17. In the latter stages three boats were taking part.
At 1925 B/17, a motor torpedo boat was seen firing red recognition flares close to the southward. HMS Urge dived for 1.5 hours.
At 2345 B/17, an aircraft dropped a flare fairly close. The aircraft then flew directly overhead at low altitude. Urge then dived for 15 minutes. (21)

18 Dec 1941 At 0400 B/18, south of Cape dell Armi, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted the track of a torpedo just ahead. Urge crash dived. HE of motor torpedo boats was picked up. Between one and three were patrolling in the area until 0630 B/18. They were also heard to be using Asdics.
HMS Urge ran to the southward during the day to clear the area.
At 2000 B/18, the now surfaced HMS Urge was put down for 55 minutes due to patrolling motor torpedo boats.
At 2214 B/18, a signal was passed to Capt. S.10 that HMS Urge had to withdraw from her patrol position due to enemy A/S activity. (21)
19 Dec 1941 At 0100 B/19, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) set course to return to her patrol position.
At 0240 B/19, HMS Urge dived for a motor torpedo boat which fired white Very lights.
At 0400 B/19, HMS Urge surfaced and continued her passage northwards.
At 0440 B/19, HMS Urge had to dive for an aircraft which dropped a flare fairly close. The aircraft then flew overhead and HMS Urge dived.

At 0820 B/19, Capt. S.10's signal timed 0351 B/19 was received which ordered HMS Urge to patrol in position 37°30'N, 15°58'E. Course was set accordingly and HMS Urge arrived in her new patrol position at 1230 B/19.
At 1730 B/19, HMS Urge is ordered to leave patrol and return to Malta.
At 1900 B/19, HMS Urge set course to return to Malta.
At 1930 B/19, HMS Urge was forced to dive for 15 minutes when an aircraft flare was dropped near her.
At 1947 B/19, just after surfacing a motor torpedo boat was heard close by. Urge again dived, now for half an hour.
At 2015 B/19, HMS Urge surfaced and continued her passage southwards. (21)
20 Dec 1941 Around 1100 B/20, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She had arrived off Malta around 0910 B/20 but 20 minutes later she had to dive for half an hour as an air raid was in progress and bombs were being dropped.
At 1015 B/20, a second was of enemy bombers was seen approaching Malta and HMS Urge again had to dive but now for 20 minutes. (21)

29 Dec 1941 HMS Upholder, with Lt. C.P. Norman, RN temporary in command (to give Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn a rest during her next patrol) and HMS Urge (with Lt. J.D. Martin, RN temporary in command (to give Lt.Cdr. Tomkinson a rest during her next patrol) conducted exercises off Malta with HMS Beryl.

When returning from the exercises around 1630 B/29, they were attacked by German Me 109 fighter aircraft of 6 JG/53. HMS Urge dived fairly quickly but some shells hit the casing causing some minor damage, but on HMS Upholder the crew on the bridge were reading a signal from a shore signal station and did not notice the approaching aircraft in time (the message was to warn Upholder about the approaching aircraft). Lt. Norman was hit by gunfire from one of the German fighters and was wounded. Despite his wounds he managed to shut the top hatch as Upholder crash dived. When Upholder arrived back at Malta he was taken to hospital and Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn assumed command again, his rest during Upholders next patrol being cancelled.

30 Dec 1941 Around 1835 B/30, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the East coast of Tunisia between Kuriat and Lampion Island, (Area K; 35°25'N, 11°10'E, 345° - 40 - 70).
Spare Commanding Officer Martin had taken temporary command of HMS Urge in order to give Lt.Cdr. Tomkinson a rest.
31 Dec 1941 At 1030 B/31, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) entered her patrol area in position 35°40'N, 12°40'E. She commenced to patrol on the trade route between Pantelleria and Tripoli. (21)

1 Jan 1942 At 0450 B/1, while near Lampione Island, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a small vessel at extreme range down moon. This ship was steering 120° at 8 knots. Urge attempted to close but when four points on the quarter of the vessels, which then looked like a small destroyer, turned towards. Urge then dived. No HE was picked up. [However no destroyer or torpedo boat was in area at the time. Perhaps it was a smaller vessel which we were unable to identify.]
At 2210 B/1, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1245 B/1, was received which reported a merchant vessel with one destroyer as escort expected to pass Kuriat the next morning. Course was altered to intercept.

The info was apparently not from an ULTRA signal. Perhaps, a Mounier network (spy network in Tunisia) report. Possibly this referred to the German transport Sturla (1397 GRT, built 1933). She had sailed from Palermo for Sousse where she arrived at 0800 B/1, she sailed from Sousse at 2000 B/1 (perhaps earlier than anticipated) and was to meet the torpedo boat Calliope off Kerkennah buoy no.4 at 0800 B/2. Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 1800 B/2 and was at the rendezvous on time. She sighted Sturla but it took an hour to ascertain her identity as the German ship was flying the French flag with French markings. (21)

2 Jan 1942 Around 2339 B/2, east of Mahdia, Tunisia HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a ship bearing 240° proceeding on course 160°. Urge closed to investigate. The vessel proved to be a 3-masted schooner proceeding southwards. The vessel was not laden and was proceeding under her own power with a following wind. Thought this may be a patrolling 'Q-ship'. Urge retired to seaward. [We have been unable to identify this vessel which may well have been Vichy-French.] (21)

4 Jan 1942 At 2121 B/4, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), which is now patrolling off Lampione Island, received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1537 B/4, which ordered her to patrol in position 32°50'N, 14°37'E (north-west of Misrathah, Libya). Course was set accordingly.
At 1402 B/5, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1007 B/5, was received which ordered HMS Urge to return to her original patrol position. (21)

5 Jan 1942 At 2035 B/5, when to the east-south-east of Lampedusa, in position 35°21'N, 13°02'E, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), observed a large flash and loud explosion bearing 200° at a range of 3 miles. The A/S operator reported suspected HE on about the same bearing but nothing was seen. HMS Urge dived for half an hour. (21)

5 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1057 B/5, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that two enemy destroyers had been sighted at 1015 B/5 to the north-east of Khoms, Libya in position 32°49'N, 14°41'E proceeding on course 205° at 15 knots.

In a signal timed 1213 B/5, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS Upright and HMS Urge then an enemy battleship with three cruisers and four destroyers had been reported that morning to the north-east of Khoms, Libya in position 32°59'N, 14°35'E proceeding on course 70° at 15 knots. This most likely referred to the close cover force for a convoy. This close cover force was made up of the battleship Caio Duilio, light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta, Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo and the destroyers Maestrale, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Scirocco and Lanzerotto Malocello.

In a signal timed 1945 B/5, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright and HMS Urge of the following; Small 9 knots and medium 7 knots merchant ship(s) close west of Tripoli at 1800 B/5. Anticipate they are using route past Turgeuness and arriving Kerkenah about noon 6th. All merchant ships encountered are to be attacked. (31)
6 Jan 1942 At 0145 B/6, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1945 B/5 (see the event for 5 January 1942). Urge altered course to intercept.

At 0755 B/6, it was clear now that the position in which to intercept the enemy ships could not be reached in time. Lt. Martin decided to patrol off Lampione Island during the day and shift to the western end of the patrol area during the night. (21)

6 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1523 B/6, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that a large merchant ship and one destroyer had been sighted at 1000 B/6, in position 36°09'N, 10°40'E proceeding on course 109° at 10 knots. Aircraft will endeavour to attack north of Kerkennah after dark tonight.

This was the Italian transport Delia (5406 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the torpedo boat Cigno. She arrived in Sousse at 1100 B/6 due to the bad weather. She had sailed at 2345 B/4-2400 B/4 from Palermo for Tripoli, escorted by the torpedo boat Cigno. The torpedo boat was supposed to leave her at dawn on the 6th but escorted her as far as Sousse. At 1020 B/6, when 10 miles from Sousse, Cigno observed a Blenheim bomber (most certainly the one which reported them). At 1800 B/6, the torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Tripoli to escort Delia from Kerkenah buoy no.5. They joined there at 0800 B/7. At 1125 B/7, they were attacked by a single Wellington bomber which was shot down by Calliope. They arrived at Tripoli at 0840 B/8. (31)

6 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 2357 B/6, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that at 2300 B/6 an aircraft had reported two medium merchant vessels and two destroyers in position 35°47'N. 11°23'E proceeding on course 360° at 8 knots. They were also informed that aircraft were at that moment taking off from Malta to attack the reported enemy ships.

This must have referred to the Italian transport Perla (5741 GRT, built 1926) on passage from Tripoli to Trapani escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cascino. The Perla was sunk early on the 7th by aircraft torpedoes in position 215° - Pantelleria - 35 miles. (31)
7 Jan 1942 At 0635 B/7, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) commenced patrolling of Mahdia, Tunisia.

At 2020 B/7, in position 35°29'N, 11°18'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 180°, range one mile. Enemy course was 000°. Urge dived to avoid being sighted. At 2140 B/7 Urge surfaced and resumed patrolling.

Possibly this was the torpedo boat Orsa which had earlier been escorting the transport Probitas (Italian, 5663 GRT, built 1919). They had sailed from Tripoli at 1445 B/6 for Trapani. Orsa reported to be off Ras Kapoudia at 1915 B/7 and changed course to Pantelleria as Probitas had engine defects and had trouble in the heavy seas. Earlier that day Orsa had hunted a supposed enemy submarine after a torpedo attack had been reported. No Allied submarine however made an attack at that time so the attack must have been bogus. (21)
9 Jan 1942 At 0200 B/9, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 B/8 was received recalling HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to Malta. At that moment she was 7 miles to the north-west of Lampione Island. (21)
11 Jan 1942 Around 0800 B/11, HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
At 0520 B/11, HMS Urge had to dive in position 235° - Delimara Light - 4.5 miles (south-west of Malta) for what is thought to be an approaching motor torpedo boat. (21)

22 Jan 1942 Around 2300 B/22, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the approaches of Tripoli in position 32°56'N, 14°32'E to intercept an important enemy convoy.

The convoy referred to was made up of the transports Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940), Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941), Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) and Victoria (Italian, 13098 GRT, built 1931) en-route from Italy to Tripoli (Ravello returned to Messina due to defects). They were escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Antonio da Noli, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the torpedo boats Orsa and Castore.

A close cover force for this convoy was made up of the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta, Muzio Attendolo and Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Carabiniere and Fuciliere.
A distant cover force for this convoy was made up of the battleship Duilio and the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Alfredo Oriani, Scirocco and Ascari.

This was Operation T 18: The Italian 7th Cruiser Squadron (Duca d’Aosta, etc. under Admiral De Courten) sailed from Taranto at 0947 B/22-1112 B/22 and returned at 1520 B/25-1625 B/25. It was unsuccessfully attacked by HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO, RN) at 1347 C/22 (Italians: 1450 B/22) and joined the convoy at 2142 B/23 (probably to reinforce the AA defence after the air attack), The Duilio squadron (under Admiral Bergamini) sailed from Taranto screening Victoria at 1545 B/22-1624 B/22 and returned at 0610 B/25-0800 B/25 (except Ascari which picked up survivors of Victoria and went to Tripoli).

They were actually two convoys: (1) Monviso, Vettor Pisani and Ravello escorted by the destroyers Carabiniere, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Antonio Da Noli, Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello and the torpedo boat Castore sailed from Messina at 0700 B/22-0800 B/22 (the torpedo Orsa had sailed from Naples at 1735 B/21 to join the convoy, both torpedo boats were equipped with the German S-gerat (sonar), for Tripoli where they arrived at 1430 B/24-1715 B/24. Ravello had steering gear defects and had to turn back shortly after sailing. (2) The liner (troop transport) Victoria sailed with the Duilio squadron.

The two convoys merged at 1800 B/22. At 1730 B/23, three RAF torpedo bombers attacked and Victoria was hit astern on the starboard side, she was hit by two more torpedoes at 1840 B/23 and 1850 B/23 and sank at 1910 B/23, 1064 survivors were picked up by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera and Ascari of the 1455 on board and arrived at Tripoli at 1602 B/24-1630 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli at 1841 B/23-1845 B/23 to reinforce the escort of the convoy. At 0859 B/24, the convoy was unsuccessfully attacked by HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and Geniere, Malocello and Castore hunted the submarine.
At 0735 B/24, the Seventh Cruiser Squadron left the convoy to return to Taranto.
At 1440 B/24, the Duilio squadron left the convoy to return to Taranto.

23 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 0203 B/23, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) that a battleship, a liner, three destroyers and two unknown vessels proceeding on course 180° at 15 knots had been sighted of Cape Colleone at 0105 B/23.
In a signal timed 1725 B/23 the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Urge and HMS P 36 to proceed with all dispatch by day, estimated enemy will pass your positions around 0200 B/24.
In a signal timed 1740 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; Aircraft will attack with bombs and torpedoes about midnight. No friendly surface force will be near you.

In a signal timed 2010 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; one battleship, three cruisers, sixteen destroyers and four merchant vessels were seen at 1740 B/23 in position 33°34'N, 16°52'E proceeding on course 240° at 15 knots.
In a signal timed 2337 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; Position, course and speed of the convoy at 2020 B/23 was 33°15'N, 16°45'E, 187°, 14 knots. (31)
24 Jan 1942 At 0207 B/24, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position. Having proceeded with all despatch since 1830 B/23.

At 0532 B/24, aircraft flares were seen bearing 080° and at 0550 B/24 a torpedo hit was heard. This was probably the transport Victoria (Italian, 13098 GRT, built 1931) being hit and sunk by British Albacore torpedo aircraft.
At 0710 B/24, HMS Urge dived.
At 0753 B/24, HE was picked up and masts were seen to the southward believed to be three merchant vessels, three Condottieri-class cruisers and four destroyers, all spread out.
At 0859 B/24, a torpedo hit was heard which was thought to be HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) attacking which was indeed correct.
At 1900 B/24, HMS Urge set course for Malta in accordance with the recall signal that had been received in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1040 B/24. She was to arrive at Malta at dawn on the 26th. (32)
26 Jan 1942 Around 0730 B/26, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) arrived at Malta.
She departed again to resume her patrol around 1915 B/26. She was now to patrol in an area off the east coast of Sicily from Syracuse to the Straits of Messina. (32)
27 Jan 1942 At 0700 B/27, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) commenced to patrol in position 37°54'N, 15°25'E to intercept enemy submarines operating from Augusta. (32)

29 Jan 1942 At 0421 B/29, east of Syracuse, in position 37°04'N, 13°34'E, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 080° at a range of about 4 nautical miles. She was down moon from HMS Urge. The submarines course was approximately 350°. Urge closed at 9 knots for 10 minutes. During this time the enemy had turned end on, which was thought to be an evasive action, and as a result the range did not decrease. As HMS Urge was up moon the attack was broken off.
This was the Italian submarine Platino returning to Augusta from patrol. (32)

31 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1403 B/31, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) to return to Malta by the north-east route arriving at 0730 B/3. She was to leave patrol by proceeding through position 37°00'N, 15°35'E.

She was also informed that HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) would leave position 35°51'N, 15°15'E about 2330 B/2 for position 37°15'N, 16°10'E. Submarines encountered on opposite course after 0001 B/3 were not to be attacked. (31)

1 Feb 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) attacked an enemy convoy off Capo dell Armi, Calabria, Italy in position 37°56'N, 15°42'E. A hit is claimed but in fact all torpedoes missed the target.

At 0710 B/31-0755 B/31, the convoy was made up of the Italian tanker Rondine (6077 GRT, 1924) and the German transport Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) and had left Taranto escorted by the Italian destroyer Nicoloso da Recco and the torpedo boat Aretusa and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which sailed from Messina at 0340 B/1 and joined them off Cape Spartivento. They arrived at Messina at 1345 B/01.
Two torpedoes were observed to miss Rondine only 20 metres astern. Aretusa dropped depth charges and claimed to have seriously damaged the submarine.
1015 B/1 - Heard a distant depth charge.
1025 B/1 - Another distant depth charge was heard. Also sighted a flying boat to the Eastward. Looked like something was coming towards Urge.

1027 B/1 - Sighted the upperworks of a ship that was hugging the coast. Shortly afterwards it was seen to be a convoy of a 3500 tons merchant vessel and two small merchant vessel of about 1000 tons escorted by three destroyers. Started attack.
1053 B/1 - Fired three torpedoes from 7000 yards at the 3500 tons merchant vessel.
1057 B/1 - Heard one hit.
1058 B/1 - Heard one torpedo hitting the rocks. A counter attack with 29 depth charges now followed.

1130 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth to find one destroyer circling round to position where the target had been hit. No other ships were in sight. Withdrew to the South-East and reloaded the torpedo tubes. (32)
2 Feb 1942 At 1950 B/2, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (32)
3 Feb 1942 Around 0730 B/3, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (32)

12 Feb 1942 Around 1930 B/12, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform two special operations and to patrol to the north of Sicily.

16 Feb 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) landed one of the three agents she had to land near Tunis, Tunisia. The landing of the other two agents had to be abandoned due to the worsening weather.

The agent who landed was Philippe “Toto” Saillard, who had been trained in Malta as a radio operator. This was his second mission to Tunisia. He had already been landed by HMS Utmost (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) on 26 October 1941. On 29 November 1941, he had been picked up by the fast motor torpedo boat XMAS (former Italian MAS 452, captured during the ill-fated raid on Malta of 26 July 1941). Saillard would be in contact with Malta until the liberation of Tunisia in May 1943.
For more info on special operations in Tunisia see the following website this website and this book (both offsite links).
2020 B/16 - Sent in the Folbot with Capt. Wilson and one of the three agents. A slight swell made this difficult.

2125 B/16 - The Folbot returned half full of water but had to be abandoned when she broke while being hoisted aboard. Due to the weather the landing of the other two agents had to be abandoned for this night. May be the next night would bring calmer weather.
2130 B/16 - Withdrew to the centre of the Gulf of Tunis.
[A report by Capt. Wilson on this special operation could not be located at the National Archives in London.] (32)

17 Feb 1942 During the evening, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), again tried to land the two remaining agents but the weather was still not suitable. It was decided to postpone the landing for another day. (32)

18 Feb 1942 During the day Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN noticed that the weather became even worse and that it would be impossible to land the two remaining agents. Course was set to the North of Sicily where a parcel had to be delivered to another agent. Weather conditions became even worse and it was decided to remain in the Gulf of Tunis to ride out this gale on the surface. (32)

18 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1650 B/18, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN); If project A not completed, abandon and withdraw informing me as arranged. Then establish patrol on Gallo - St. Vito route. Orders will follow on receipt of your signal. (33)
19 Feb 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) was now able to proceed towards the north of Sicily and course was set accordingly. (32)

19 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1921 B/19, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) notified Capt. S.10 that the first possible night to land the package would be during the night of 20/21 February.

In a signal timed 2313 B/19, Capt. S.10 did not approve this and ordered that 'project D' should be carried out between 0200 B/22 and 0400 B/22 during the first possible night (21/22 Feb.) or one hour later on the following night (22/23 Feb.). (34)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 0940 B/20, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) that a German U-Boat was suspected west of Marittimo Island in position 38°00'N, 11°00'E at 0235 B/20.
No German U-boat was however in that area at this time. (33)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1957 B/21, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) that reconnaissance had shown that five destroyers and four medium sized merchant vessels were present at Palermo. (33)

22 Feb 1942 At 0100 B/22, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) started to close the landing position to the east of Cape St. Vito. This was the same agent that was to be contacted on 1 October 1941 when it looked like the enemy was patrolling the area trying to lure the submarine into a trap. HMS Urge came into the Gulf about 3 miles off the land proceeding at 2 knots well trimmed down, listening on Asdics and with an extra lookout on the bridge.

At 0140 B/22, when about 3 miles north-west of Cala Rossa, HE was picked up on the Asdics on a bearing of 070°. Shortly afterwards a motor torpedo boat was seen on this bearing proceeding slowly southwards.
At 0142 B/22, HMS Urge dived and proceeded slowly out of the Gulf on course 340°. The E-boat was heard proceeding down the coast in spasms and eventually stopped on the bearing of Calo Rossa.
At 0214 B/22, HE was heard again on the same bearing as earlier.
At 0405 B/22, Fast HE was heard to the south-east lasting for about ten minutes.

At 0657 B/22, an A/S trawler was seen coming out of the Gulf on a course of about 060° and about 2 miles from the shore. It appeared as if this vessel had also been near Cala Rossa. In view of all these unusual activities, it was decided to abandon the landing and shift patrol just to the west of Cape San Vito. Urge then proceeded westwards along the coastal traffic route during the day. A few trawlers were seen patrolling in the area during the day.

The agent Alfred Rossi had been captured by the Italians in October 1941 after being landed by HMS Urge (when Sub-Lt Brian Lloyd had been killed, see the events for 1 October 1941 and 24 September 1941 for more info) and was now operating under their control, feeding Malta with false information. The motor torpedo boats MAS 575 and MAS 576 sailed from Trapani at 0530 B/22 for an A/S sweep but there is no indication if they were actually hunting for HMS Urge. The German transport Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the torpedo boat Polluce had sailed from Palermo at 1500 B/22 but had to take refuge in Trapani due to the bad weather (it is a bit surprising that she sailed from Palermo if a British submarine was known to be in the vicinity. Perhaps the Italian Secret Service and the Italian Navy did not communicate a lot between them.
At 1945 B/22, fast HE was heard on bearing 350°. Thought to be a destroyer proceeding at high speed.
At 1955 B/22, more HE was heard behind the original HE.

At 1957 B/22, Urge surfaced and saw very dimly a largish ship with a destroyer ahead and possible also one astern about 5 miles to the northward. Enemy course was about 090°. They passed about 6.5 miles north of Cape San Vito.

At 2130 B/22, a signal was received ordering HMS Urge to leave patrol around noon the following day and return to Malta. This was Capt. S.10's signal timed 0935 B/22 which gave the following; Whether project D complete or not leave Gallo - Vito patrol 1200 B/23 to make passage QBB 65 during daylight following day thence by S.E. Channel to arrive Marsamxett (Malta) 0715 B/26. HMS Olympus (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Dymott, RN) leaves 37°54'N, 11°53'E about 0200 B/24 to N.W. You are to keep North and East of position 'L' until 0500 B. (34)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (33)
23 Feb 1942 At 1945 B/23, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), after having patrolled 5 miles north-east of Cape St. Vito, left patrol to return to Malta. (32)

23 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1847 B/23, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Olympus (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Dymott, RN) that an Italian submarine was suspected in the vicinity of position 38°05'N, 09°00'E at 0300 B/23.
The signal was also repeated to HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN)
No Italian submarine was in that area around this time though. (33)

24 Feb 1942 At 1010 B/24, when about 15 miles south of Favignana Island, two destroyers were sighted believed to be the Italian Maestrale and Antonio Pigafetta. They were proceeding northwards at around 24 knots. At the same time smoke was seen coming from between Marittimo and Favignana Islands. It was hoped this was a southbound convoy from Palermo so no action was taken against the destroyers. In fact the southbound ships were an old destroyer escorting two small ships of about 600 tons. Again smoke was sighted to the north and it was decided not to fire at the small ships in case the two destroyers were going to escort southward an important ship.

The destroyers sighted at 1010 B/24 were indeed the two they were thought to be. They had sailed from Tripoli at 1730 B/23-1845 B/23 and arrived at Palermo at 1400 B/24. They were returning to Italy after escorting a convoy to Libya.
At 1130 B/24, it was seen that the origin of the smoke was a tug towing a schooner to Marittimo. Urge remained patrolling in the area until 1800 B/24 but no convoy came her way.
At 1430 B/24, a trawler and two schooner minesweepers passed going north.
[We have been unable to identify the the other ships seen on this day. Note that the torpedo boat Polluce sailed at 0656 B/24 from Trapani to Palermo but was apparently not seen.] (32)
26 Feb 1942 Around 0700 B/26, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was then taken in hand at the Malta Dockyard where the port generator was replaced. (32)

9 Mar 1942 Around 1945 B/9, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern part of the Tyrrhenian Sea.
The following programme was intended; leave Malta 9th, Marittimo 11th, Naples 15th, Policastro 17th, Vaticano 18th, Stromboli 19th, Gallo 20th, Vito 21st arriving Malta A.M./24th.

10 Mar 1942 At 2145 B/10, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1859 B/10 which reported that a large, damaged enemy merchant ship was being towed from Pantelleria to Trapani. It was expected the ship would arrive at Trapani around 2300 B/10. Urge increased speed to full to try to intercept.

This must have referred to the damaged Italian transport Luciano Manara (8103 GRT, built 1942) which had been attacked near Pantelleria by three Albacores and three Swordfish aircraft in which she sustained damage from a torpedo. The ship was on passage from Trapani to Tripoli and was escorted by the Italian destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the Italian torpedo-boat Polluce.

They came under air attack between 2120 B/9-2325 B/9. Manara was torpedoed at 2326 hours on the starboard bow but managed to proceed under her own power and they reached Pantelleria at 0053 B/10. They sailed from Pantelleria at 0950 B/10, at 1430 B/10, the tug Nereo arrived from Trapani to assist, Usodimare ordered her to follow (just in case she would be needed). They reached Palermo at 0135 B/11-0241 B/11.

' Force K ' from Malta, made up of the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyer HMS Kingston (Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) was also deployed to attack the convoy but failed to find it. (32)

11 Mar 1942 At 0035 B/11, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted flares bearing 310°, distant about 10 to 15 miles. Nothing was seen of the damaged ship but a loud explosion was heard at about 0220 B/11.
At 0340 B/11, a destroyer was sighted going southwards at high speed towards Pantelleria.

The Italian destroyer Antonio da Noli and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade were escorting the transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941) from Tripoli to Palermo. So they were going northward and not southward. Monviso and Da Noli arrived at Palermo at 0215 B/11 but Pallade only arrived at 1042 B/11. Perhaps Pallade had briefly turned back for some reason but we can not confirm this. No other destroyers / torpedo boats were known to have been in the area at that time.
At 0535 B/11 - HMS Urge passed Marittimo Island and set course for Naples.

At 1950 B/11, on surfacing the port generator, which had only recently been replaced, was found to be defective. At 2030 B/11, due to the defect course was reversed to return to Malta. At 2150 B/11, a signal was passed to Capt. S.10 informing him of this. (32)
12 Mar 1942 At 0421 B/12, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) dived for 20 minutes to evade a destroyer patrolling off Marittimo.
At 0830 B/12, three minesweepers en-route from Trapani to Pantelleria were sighted proceeding on course 200°. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
At 2300 B/12, a very powerful searchlight was sweeping to seawards from Cape San Marco, Sicily for about 40 minutes. (32)

13 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1029 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) the enemy motor torpedo boat activity was suspected between Gozo and Grand Harbour during the night of 12/13 March until dawn. (35)
14 Mar 1942 Around 0700 B/14, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (32)

23 Mar 1942 Around 0530 B/23, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern part of the Tyrrhenian Sea south-east of the line Marittimo - Naples. On board was also a party which was to try to wreck a train.

24 Mar 1942 At 2100 B/24, south-west of Marsala, Sicily, in position 37°30'N, 12°10'E, two northbound schooners were seen by HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN). Lt.Cdr. Tomkinson did not attack as to not compromise the route taken by the submarine. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (36)

26 Mar 1942 At 2300 B/26, south-south-east of Capri, in position 40°22'N, 14°20'E, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted a schooner laying stopped to the east. Also an unidentified small ship was seen to the northward. The schooner was not attacked so as to not give away the submarine's presence. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (36)
27 Mar 1942 During the day, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted several small schooners tunny fishing in Salerno Bay. (32)

27 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 0917 B/27, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) that there was a possibility of a U-Boat being encountered which was on passage from Messina to La Spezia.
This must have referred to the German submarine U-83 which was on passage from Salamis to La Spezia but she was already in the vicinity of Naples when the signal was sent. (35)

28 Mar 1942 At 1100 B/28, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted a destroyer and a flying boat patrolling about 10 miles to the south-west of the Bocca Piccola. Again several tunny fishing schooners were seen in the neighbourhood.
At 1330 B/28, the destroyer and flying boat returned to Naples Bay keeping to the northward of Capri Island.
This was the torpedo boat Pegaso which sailed from Naples for sonar exercises at 0920 B/28 and returned at 1520 B/28. (32)
29 Mar 1942 During the night of 29 / 30 March 1942, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) landed an SBS (Special Boat Service) raiding party to wreck a train. This was successful.

An enemy ship was sighted shortly afterwards and attacked with three torpedoes that all missed. A gun action followed in which three hits were obtained. As the enemy gunfire drew nearer and straddled the submarine the action had to be broken off.

This was the Italian merchant vessel Pugliola (2974 GRT, built 1917) on passage from Messina to Naples; she was slightly damaged by a single shell and two rounds of MG fire. She returned fire with two rounds (not three as claimed by Tomkinson) and forced the submarine to submerge. Two auxiliary patrol boats Angelini and Eolo were sent to hunt the submarine but they were not equipped with hydrophones and they soon abandoned the chase.

29 March 1942 2335 B/29 - Arrived to within half a mile of the shore just north of Pisciotta, Italy. The Folbot party of two (Lt. T.G.A. Walker of Hertforshire regiment and Sgt. H.H.V. Penn) cast off and arrived on the shore 15 minutes later.
30 March 1942 0032 B/30 - The Folbot party returned. They had successfully placed their charge under the railway line.

0055 B/30 - An electric train hit the charge. The engine blew up and came falling down the embankment. During the approach of the train a ship of about 3000 tons was sighted to the seaward. Enemy course was 320° and she was about 3 nautical miles from the land.
0056 B/30 - In position 40°04'N, 15°07'5"E fired three torpedoes from 3500 yards. All three torpedoes missed as the enemy most likely saw the tracks. The gun was now manned and a chase started.
0114 B/30 - Fire was opened at a range of 2000 to 2500 yards. 16 Rounds were fired for 3 hits.
0118 B/30 - The enemy meanwhile had also opened fire and was now straddling Urge so it was decided to break off the action and dive. (32)

1 Apr 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian light cruiser Giovanni delle Bande Nere 11 nautical miles south-east of Stromboli, Italy. Two torpedo hits were obtained.

The cruiser had been on passage from Messina to la Spezia, escorted by the destroyer Aviere and the torpedo boat Libra. A third escort, the destroyer Fuciliere, had just turned back because of defects. Of her passengers and crew, 381 were lost. A large scale rescue operation was quickly organised, the hospital ship Capri, the auxiliary Lago Tana and the torpedo boats Pallade and Centauro joining Aviere and Libra and rescuing a total of 391 survivors.
0830 B/1 - Sighted a flying boat coming up from Messina along the route to Naples. Went to 85 feet.
0841 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth after hearing faint HE. Sighted the fore top of a warship.
0844 B/1 - Identified the warship as a 8" cruiser with an escort of two destroyers steering about 330° at 21 knots. Started attack.
0854 B/1 - In position 38°37'5"N, 15°22'E fired four torpedoes from 5000 yards. One hit was obtained. [Two according to Italian sources.]
0907 B/1 - The first depth charges were dropped out of a total of 38 but none was close. Breaking up noises were heard.
0940 B/1 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the two destroyers and three flying boats. There was no sign of the cruiser. Urge went deep again and withdrew to the westward.
2200 B/1 - Passed a signal to Capt. S.10 reporting the sinking of the cruiser. (32)

3 Apr 1942 While patrolling off the north coast of Sicily, between Cape Gallo and Cape St. Vito, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted an Italian destroyer and three German R-boats during the day. All were westbound. [The times these ships were sighted are not noted in the patrol report and as HMS Urge was lost later in this month no log is available as this went down with the submarine.]
At 2017 B/3, on surfacing, HMS Urge left patrol and set course to Malta.

The R-boats sighted on this day must have been the R-9, R-14 and R-15 from the 6th R-boat Flotilla which were on passage from Messina (departed 0800 B/3) to Trapani (arrived 1845 B/3). [Note: Supermarina states that they sailed at 0455 B/3 and arrived at 1830 B/3.]
[We have been unable to identify this 'Italian destroyer / torpedo boat', none can be placed in this area at this time according to the documents available.] (37)

5 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1808 B/5, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed that HMS Havock (Lt.Cdr. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) would be sailing at 2000 B/5 by the south east swept channel thence direct to position 36°42.5'N, 11°04'E. Speed of advance would be 28 knots. The submarines were instructed to keep clear. (38)
6 Apr 1942 Around 0745 B/6, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (32)

11 Apr 1942 Around 0530 B/11, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of Pantelleria up to Lampione Island. (32)

11 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2155 B/11, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) that at 2100 B/11 a transport (motor vessel) and a destroyer had been sighted in position 33°19'N, 12°53'E, proceeding on course 345° at 15 knots.
This must have referred to the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) which had departed Tripoli around 1900 B/11 for Naples escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro.

It is to be expected that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would try to intercept this ship during the night of 11/12 April. No attacks were however reported by the Italians though it is always possible that one was made which was not observed.

Having either made a failed attack on this convoy or having failed to intercept this convoy at all it seems likely that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would proceeded further to the south. It would be logical for him to proceed to the south-east part of area T as HMS Upholder would now be near the eastern edge anyway. Enemy convoys leaving Tripoli would most likely all proceed through the south-eastern part of area T before taking one of several possible convoy routes. Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn had also patrolled that area as recently as February 1942 and had succeeded in sinking an enemy transport back then.

HMS Urge was en-route to patrol in the area between Pantelleria and Lampione Islands. An area where the reported convoy was expected through proceed through so HMS Urge continued her passage. While still en route to this area HMS Urge encountered a southbound convoy late in the morning of the 12th and attacked it. [See the page of HMS Urge for the details.] HMS Urge made no direct enemy report on this convoy for just after the attack a large modern transport was seen proceeding to the north-west. This must have been the Gino Allegri and her escort. [There is no mention of an escort in the patrol report of HMS Urge which never came closer then 5 to 6 nautical miles.] At 2250 B/12, HMS Urge finally cleared a signal to Capt. S.10 giving details of her exploits that day and also reporting the southbound convoy.
Apparently no signal was sent to HMS Upholder regarding this southbound convoy so she must have been unaware of it. (38)
12 Apr 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) made a torpedo attack on an enemy convoy about 30 nautical mile south-east of Pantelleria. No hits were obtained.

According to Italian sources these were the Italian transports Amsterdam (Italian, 10873 GRT, built 1921) and Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the destroyer Italian destroyer Premuda and the Italian torpedo boat Clio. From Giulia three torpedo tracks were observed and from Clio two explosions were heard.

The convoy sailed from Trapani (originally from Naples via Palermo) at 2235 B/11-2240 B/11 for Tripoli where they arrived at 0931 B/13-0956 B/13. The convoy was reinforced by the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari and German R-boats (motor minesweepers) R 12, R 9 and R 15 which sailed from Tripoli at 0230 B/13-0245 B/13 and joined at 0635 B/13.
0718 B/12 - Heard distant depth charging from now on.
1105 B/12 - Heard HE.

1108 B/12 - In position 36°21'N, 12°39'E sighted two merchant ships of about 7000 tons bearing 005°. Distance was about 8000 yards. The escort consisted of two destroyers and two flying boats. Started attack.

1120 B/12 - Fired four torpedoes from 5000 yards. Just after firing a flying boat passed over Urge and she must have sighted the tracks and warned the convoy as HE stopped immediately and the ships started maneuvering. No torpedo hits were therefore obtained.
1143 B/12 - Returned to periscope depth to find the convoy steaming south unscathed. (32)

12 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2252 B/12, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN).
They were to take up the following positions by midnight during the night of 14/15 April.
HMS Urge proceed through position 35°00'N, 13°10'E to position 'X' which was 33°11'N, 14°09'E.
HMS Upholder proceed through position 33°25'N, 13°10'E to position 'Y' which was 33°10'N, 14°23'E.
HMS Thrasher proceed through position 32°50'N, 16°00'E to position 'Y' which was 33°09'N, 14°37'E.

Object of interception is an important convoy, expected mean line of advance 235° through position 'X' about dawn on the 15th. If forced to retire HMS Urge was to keep west of 14°15'E, HMS Thrasher east of 14°30'E and HMS Upholder between these longitudes.

In a signal timed 1026 B/13, Capt. S.10 informed the submarines that the mean line of advance of the convoy would be 235° through position 'Y' about dawn on the 15th. Apparently there had been a mistake in the original signal.
In a signal timed 2355 B/14, HMS Thasher was ordered to patrol in position Z - 335° - 5 nautical miles and HMS Urge in position X - 115° - 5 nautical miles.

The important convoy referred to was en-route from Italy to Tripoli. It was made up of the transports Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) and Reichenfels (German, 7744 GRT, built 1936). They had departed Naples around 0730 B/13 for Tripoli. They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolo Zeno and the torpedo boat Pegaso.

The convoy merged with two more convoys, the first convoy to join was made up of the transport Reginaldo Giuliani (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1942) which had departed Brindisi around 1000 B/13, escorted by the Italian destroyer Mitragliere and the Italian torpedo boat Aretusa. On these three convoys merging Aretusa parted company.
Later also a convoy made up of the transport Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) joined. It had had departed Taranto around 1200 B/13 escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia and Turbine.
The combined convoy arrived at Tripoli around 0930 B/15.
None of the submarines on the patrol line reported being in contact with the convoy. (38)

13 Apr 1942 At 0100 B/13, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2252 B/12. Course was set to comply with the orders in this signal. Speed was set at 9.5 knots to reach this position in time to intercept the expected convoy. (32)
15 Apr 1942 At 0001 B/15, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) is still 3 nautical miles to the north of the ordered patrol position (X = 33°11'N, 14°09'E).
HMS Urge sighted no shipping during the day. Patrolling aircraft had been sighted around 0230/0300 hours.

At 1800 B/15, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1347 B/15 was received which ordered HMS Urge to patrol to the south of Lampedusa on the 17th proceeding through positions 33°57'N, 12°54'E and 35°00'N, 12°52'E and later through position 35°31'N, 12°51'E by 0001 B/18 to area L (36°13'N, 11°42'E - 020° - 30 - 60).
At 2030 B/15, HMS Urge surfaced and proceeded on course 321° to comply with the above signal. (32)

18 Apr 1942 At 2210 B/18, north-west of Linosa Island, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted and dived for a large motor torpedo boat on course 130° at 18 to 20 knots. At 2239 B/18, HMS Urge surfaced and proceeded to the westward keeping 5 miles to the south of the traffic route.

Supermarina reports that the motor torpedo boat MAS 557 (the 18 April entry states MAS 567 but this appears to be an error and corrected the following day), MAS 560, MAS 563 and MAS 564 were at Pantelleria in a state of readiness at 1200 B/18. No mention of any sortie though. The motor torpedo boat sighted might thus well have been one of the above as no other MAS boats were known to be in the area. (32)

19 Apr 1942 At 2345 B/19, south-south-east of Pantellaria, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) sighted and dived for a patrolling motor torpedo boat. HMS Urge surfaced after one hour and fifteen minutes.

Supermarina reports that MAS 557, MAS 560, MAS 563 and MAS 564 were at Pantelleria in a state of readiness at 1200 B/19. No mention of any sortie though. The motor torpedo boat sighted might thus well have been one of the above as no other MAS boats were known to be in the area. (32)

20 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1654 B/20, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed by Capt. S.10 that their position was probably compromised by sighting. (38)
20 Apr 1942 Between 0217 B/20 and 0234 B/20, 7 boats of the 3rd German S-Boat Flotilla, S 61, S 54, S 56, S 33, S 34, S 31 and S 58 laid minefield MT 13 close to Valletta Harbour, Malta.
It must have been a mine from this minefield which was hit by HMS Urge in the early hours of the 27th. (39)
21 Apr 1942 At 1300 B/21, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 2117 B/20. (32)
22 Apr 1942 Around 2000 B/22, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (32)

27 Apr 1942 HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) left Malta before dawn (her sailing orders give the time as 0445 B/27) on 27 April 1942 for Alexandria. Due to the constant bombing of Malta the 10th submarine flotilla shifted his base to Alexandria. Passage was to be made via Malta's North-East Channel (course 071° from St. Elmo Light), then via (a) 36°00'N, 14°53'E (b) 35°51'N, 15°15'E (c) 34°55’N, 20°05’E (d) 34°53'N, 20°28'E (expected to pass this last point at 0700 B/30 April) (e) 32°12'N, 29°06'E thence to Alexandria. Her speed of advance was to be 90 miles per day. HMS Urge was to arrive at the Alexandria searched channel around 0530 C/6 but she failed to do so.

It has previously been claimed that the submarine was sunk by seven Italian CR.42 fighter planes from 153^Squadriglia off Ras El Hilal (Libya) on the morning of 29 April (the time was later given as 0800 hours). The claim originated with a telephone call from Comandante Dadone at Superareo to Tenente di vascello Barich at Supermarina. Dadone just stated that a submarine shelling an Axis convoy at Ras El Hilal was sighted and attacked by CR.52 (sic) fighters. Upon checking for the identity of the convoy Supermarina assumed that it was the Italian schooner San Giusto on her way to Derna as she could have been in the area. However, there was no confirmation from this vessel that she had been attacked and she was not in convoy (her file at the Ufficio Storico makes no mention of such an attack). Ras Hilal was usually visited by Alexandria-based submarines from the First Flotilla on their way to patrol off Benghazi. On 14 May 1942, San Giusto would actually be sunk by one of them (HMS Turbulent) at Ras El Hilal.

Information from Captain Manfredi to the German Naval Command in Italy stated that the convoy attacked at Ras El Hilal on 29 April consisted of three German MFPs (MFP 150, 154 and 156) and that it was attacked at 20 hours (which contradicted the earlier report, so even Manfredi appears to have been misinformed). To add to the confusion, although these three F-lighters were indeed in the area, German records do not confirm any attack although they do mention that Manfredi had reported such an attack.

Was HMS Urge sunk in this attack? This is impossible as she had sailed in the early hours of 27 April and at a speed of advance of 90 miles per day (according to her orders), her route was straight to Alexandria and she had no compelling reason to be at Ras El Hilal on 29 April. In addition she was supposed to be in 34°53'N, 20°28'E at 0700 B/30 April and this would have made her still some 150 miles short from Ras El Hilal had Tomkinson decided to go there. She could not have been at Ras El Hilal on 29 April unless Tomkinson had decided to contravene his orders and go at full speed on the surface night and day (allied submarines were instructed to travel on the surface only during the hours of darkness) and she would have had to beat speed records for “U” class submarines. Why would Tomkinson choose to risk his submarine and go to Ras El Hilal, an anchorage usually frequented by small vessels? Allied submarines had usually strict orders to follow their assigned routes otherwise they could easily be mistaken for enemy and they had more to fear from RAF bombers then from Axis aircraft, the latter having proven to be largely ineffective in fighting submarines. The CR.42 fighters of the Regia Aeronautica, armed with two 50 kg bombs, were unlikely to cause lethal damage to a submarine. The CANT Z.501 of the Regia Marina armed with two 160 kg depth charges were more apt to sink or damage a submarine and although several submarines were damaged by them, none is known to have been sunk.

Was there another submarine at Ras El Hilal? Apparently not, and submarines were frequently reported in areas where it is certain that none operated. Axis and Allied records contain many reports of attacks on non-existent submarines so the claim by the CR.42 fighters is not uncommon. In fact, at 1630 hours on 1 May, a CANT seaplane and a CA.311 fighter reported attacking a submarine just off Benghazi but made no claims of sinking. Benghazi would have made more sense than Ras El Hilal and the submarine could have been there at that time but again this is unlikely. (40)
1 May 2015 On 1 May 2015 it was incorrectly reported that the wreck of the HMS Urge had been found off Ras al Hilal, Libya.

The editor of this website and Mr. Platon Alexiades considered it likely that the wreck detected was actually that of the German submarine U-205 that foundered there while under tow by the British corvette HMS Gloxinia.

On 30 October 2019 it was announced (offsite link) that the wreck of HMS Urge has been found off Malta. The claim of her being 'found' off Ras al Hilal, Libya therefore proved to be incorrect. HMS Urge apparently fell victim to a mine laid by the German 3rd Schnellbootflottille on 20 April 1942 (MT 13 minefield). Thirty-two crew members and twelve passengers perished, there were no survivors.

May 2021 In early May 2021 it was annouced that the wreck has been dived by actual divers and not only with ROV. They were able to make a close inspection of the wreck and 100% identify the wreck as HMS Urge. The letters of her name are visibly on the Port side of the conning tower. The Ministry of Defence has since confirmed the wreck is indeed that of HMS Urge.
See this page (offsite link) for more information.
On 27 April 2022 a memorial was unveiled at Fort St. Elmo Malta.
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
